article.title: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) Promotes Plant Tolerance to Drought

events.event_label: TYLCV infection reduces transpiration, transpiration rate, and Θcrit

participants.label: transpiration rate per unit leaf area (E)

events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p039.s001: TYLCV infection in watered TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes (SVW) resulted in the reduction of transpiration, transpiration rate per unit leaf area (E), and physiological drought point (Θcrit) levels. PMC8616339.p039.s002: In infected plants subjected to drought, these values already declined slower in infected plants, rather than in uninfected. PMC8616339.p039.s003: Virus infection caused a rise in water balance stability, which may allow plants to adapt to drought.

events.event_label: TYLCV genome encodes coat protein CP, movement protein MP, Rep, and RNA suppressor
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p005.s001: Begomoviruses, the TYLCV family among them, are transmitted in nature by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci and infect a large number of agricultural crops. PMC8616339.p005.s002: TYLCV possess a single genomic molecule encapsidated in a 20 × 30 nm geminate particle. PMC8616339.p005.s003: The circular single-stranded DNA genomic molecule of about 2800 nucleotides encodes two genes, V1—or coat protein CP, and V2—or movement protein MP; the genome complementary stains encodes four genes, C1—or replication initiator protein Rep, C2—a transcription activating factor, C3—a replication enhancer, and C4—a RNA suppressor silencing contributing to virus spread.

events.event_label: drought induces soluble sugars, amino acids, and proteins
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p002.s003: To envisage the use of virus protective capacity in agriculture, TYLCV-resistant tomato lines have to be infected first with the virus before planting. PMC8616339.p002.s004: Such virus-resistant tomato plants contain virus amounts that do not cause disease symptoms, growth inhibition, or yield loss, but are sufficient to modify the metabolism of the plant, resulting in improved tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p002.s005: This phenomenon is based on the TYLCV-dependent stabilization of amounts of key osmoprotectants induced by drought (soluble sugars, amino acids, and proteins). PMC8616339.p002.s006: Although in infected TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes, stress markers also show an enhanced stability, in infected TYLCV-resistant plants, water balance and osmolyte homeostasis reach particularly high levels. PMC8616339.p002.s007: These tomato plants survive long periods of time during water withholding.

events.event_label: HSP70, BiP, and HSP90 are expressed in tomato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p036.s001: The patterns of the major members of the HSP family—HSP70, BiP, and HSP90—were followed in the leaves of uninfected and TYLCV-infected tomato lines, i.e., S-967 and R-GF967. PMC8616339.p036.s002: Withholding of water from S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes did not cause a significant increase in the abundances of HSPs (Figure 2). PMC8616339.p036.s003: Some increases in the amounts of HSP70 and HSP90 were observed, while patterns of BiP did not change. PMC8616339.p036.s004: The amounts of HSPs in uninfected S0D and R0D plants were comparable.

PMC8616339.p007.s001: TYLCV is able to manipulate the plant heat stress response and moderate the effects caused by heat on the plant. PMC8616339.p007.s002: It was proposed that one of the potential mechanisms of suppression of heat stress response was based on the ability of all six viral proteins to interact with the main heat shock transcription factor HSFA2. PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction.

events.event_label: water deficit causes accumulation of sugars and amino acids in tomatoes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p028.s001: 3.2. PMC8616339.p028.s002: Osmo-Protectants (Sugars, Amino Acids) Accumulate in TYLCV-Infected Tomatoes Grown in Conditions of Water Deficit

events.event_label: prolonged drought enhances HSPs stabilization in tomato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p036.s006: In RVD, the improvement was somehow more apparent for BiP and HSP90 (Figure 2). PMC8616339.p036.s007: These results pointed to an enhanced stabilization of HSPs patterns in leaf samples of infected tomatoes exposed to prolonged drought.

events.event_label: drought reduces activation of HSFA2, HSFA1, and HSFB1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction. PMC8616339.p007.s005: Capturing HSFA2 by viral proteins inhibited its translocation into nuclei and, consequently, downregulated the transcriptional activation of heat stress response genes. PMC8616339.p007.s006: In TYLCV-infected tomatoes exposed to drought, the activation of HSFA2 was reduced, and two other key stress transcription factors, HSFA1 and HSFB1, were also mitigated. PMC8616339.p007.s007: However, a major effect of TYLCV was the reallocation of some dominant carbohydrates and amino acids from shoots to roots, which prepared them to the effects of drought.

events.event_label: drought leads to detection of amino acids in tomato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p031.s001: The levels of several amino acids, known to be involved in response to drought, were analyzed by GC-MS, and compared between leaves of S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes exposed to drought during the 18 days-long experiment. PMC8616339.p031.s002: The amounts of proline, HO-proline, tryptophan, GABA, glutamine, valine, asparagine, and methionine were compared in S0D and SVD vs. R0D and RVD (Figure 1B). PMC8616339.p031.s003: These selected amino acids were induced by drought to varying degrees and at different periods during exposure to drought. PMC8616339.p031.s004: For example, the amounts of proline and of its derivative HO-proline increased during the entire 18-dd growth period.

PMC8616339.p043.s006: Furthermore, the CPW of R0D reached a plateau at 10 dd, while that of RVD, reached between 12–13 dd. PMC8616339.p043.s007: The CPW calculation suggests that, in plants exposed to drought, the gain of weight was due to virus infection, but not because of the identity of the treated tomato line.

events.event_label: water-deficit conditions affect shoots and roots
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p045.s006: VWR shoots lost only 15–17% weight and roots did not lose any biomass at all. PMC8616339.p045.s007: After growth in water-deficit conditions, dry weight loss of around 28% was found in shoots and 25% in roots of VDS and VDR (Figure 4).

events.event_label: drought increases abundance of protective sugars and amino acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p052.s001: The capacity of plants to resist numerous environmental stresses is associated with the increase in different cellular osmoprotectants. PMC8616339.p052.s002: The levels of protective sugars and amino acids in tomato leaves improved upon drought in non-infected R-GF967 (R0D) compared to S-967 (S0D) (Figure 1A, B). PMC8616339.p052.s003: Moreover, the patterns of most osmoprotectants analyzed, including HSPs (Figure 2), were more stable in R0D tomatoes, rather than in S0D tomatoes. PMC8616339.p052.s004: Protein homeostasis was maintained longer in TYLCV-resistant tomatoes than in susceptible tomatoes exposed to stresses.

events.event_label: soluble sugars and amino acids confer tolerance to drought
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p053.s003: Moreover, this study showed that the induction of drought tolerance is particularly pronounced in infected TYLCV-resistant plants (R-GF967), which accumulate less amounts of virus than susceptible plants, thus not affecting plant growth and development. PMC8616339.p053.s004: Our findings showed that stress-protective soluble sugars and amino acids in the leaves of uninfected, and especially in virus-infected R-GF967 tomatoes, resulted in the development of a buffering state, resulting in plant protection against drought during a prolonged time (Figure 1). PMC8616339.p053.s005: Therefore, we propose that the establishment of stress osmo-protectant stability is the main feature underlaying increased drought tolerance of TYLCV-resistant tomatoes, which is utterly improved by virus infection.

PMC8616339.p041.s001: Transpiration rate per plant weight (E) was similar in S0W and R0W tomatoes. PMC8616339.p041.s002: TYLCV infection led to their decline from the beginning of growth, in both SVW and RVW (Figure 3B). PMC8616339.p041.s003: TYLCV infection delayed the decrease in E, prolonging the water balance for several days. PMC8616339.p041.s004: If the decline of E in S0D and R0D plants started after 6 dd, in SVD and RVD, it only did so between 9 and 10 dd.

events.event_label: drought stress affects osmoprotectants (sugars and amino acids)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p111.s001: Stabilization of osmoprotectants patterns (sugars, amino acids) in R-GF967 vs. S-967 tomatoes under drought stress. PMC8616339.p111.s002: Leaf samples were collected at 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, and 18 dd after the beginning of water withholding from the four groups of experimental tomatoes R0D, RVD, S0D, and SVD. PMC8616339.p111.s003: (A) Osmo-protective sugar patterns were shown for sucrose, glucose and fructose. PMC8616339.p111.s004: The concentration of each sugar in S0D leaf the first day of sampling (0 dd) was considered as 100; the concentration of soluble sugar was calculated relative to this value.

PMC8616339.p048.s006: R0W and RVW produced eight fruits, weighing altogether about 950 g (Figure 5A), which confirmed that the virus did not influence fruit setting in the TYLCV-resistant line. PMC8616339.p048.s007: In comparison, exposure of uninfected plants to drought (R0D) also produced eight fruits but their weight decreased by about two thirds. PMC8616339.p048.s008: However, RVD plants, treated as R0D, lost only 20–25% of fruit weight (Figure 5A). PMC8616339.p048.s009: Altogether, when comparing the tomato fruits collected, it can be noticed that, although drought was associated with a smaller size of R0D tomatoes, it practically did not affect that of RVD tomatoes (Figure 5B). PMC8616339.p048.s010: Hence, TYLCV-infected R-GF967 tomatoes successfully overcame an extended cessation of irrigation, and after a recovery period, produced tomato fruits as did untreated tomato plants.

events.event_label: infection associates with PVX and PPV
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p050.s003: Some viruses are clearly beneficial to their infected host, protecting them from heat and/or water scarcity. PMC8616339.p050.s004: Increasing amounts of studies demonstrated the way viruses manage to increase the plant’s response to environmental stresses (reviewed in). PMC8616339.p050.s005: For example, four different RNA viruses, brome mosaic virus (BMV), cucumber mosaic virus (CMV), tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), and tobacco rattle virus (TRV), were shown to improve the infected plant’s tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p050.s006: Examples of plant beneficial trade-off from infection with potato virus X (PVX) and plum pox virus (PPV) was described for N. benthamiana and Arabidopsis grown under drought conditions. PMC8616339.p050.s007: Virus infection enhanced plant tolerance to drought by increasing salicylic acid amounts, in an abscisic acid (ABA)-independent manner.

events.event_label: drought increases abundance of amino acids and GABA
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p031.s001: The levels of several amino acids, known to be involved in response to drought, were analyzed by GC-MS, and compared between leaves of S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes exposed to drought during the 18 days-long experiment. PMC8616339.p031.s002: The amounts of proline, HO-proline, tryptophan, GABA, glutamine, valine, asparagine, and methionine were compared in S0D and SVD vs. R0D and RVD (Figure 1B). PMC8616339.p031.s003: These selected amino acids were induced by drought to varying degrees and at different periods during exposure to drought. PMC8616339.p031.s004: For example, the amounts of proline and of its derivative HO-proline increased during the entire 18-dd growth period. PMC8616339.p031.s006: A surprising result was obtained in uninfected R-GF967 tomatoes, where levels of proline and HO-proline exceeded that in non-infected S-967 (R0D vs. S0D). PMC8616339.p031.s007: The quantities of γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), another osmolyte protecting cells against stresses, increased upon drought but only after 12 dd (Figure 1B). PMC8616339.p031.s009: Besides GABA, three other members of glutamate family, glutamate, glutamine, and arginine, were analyzed. PMC8616339.p031.s010: Glutamic acid was not induced by drought (not shown), similar to the absence of drought-dependent induction of this amino acid in the tomato commercial cultivar Ikram, contradicting data in the literature.

PMC8616339.p057.s001: Cultivating infected R-GF967 (or resistant cultivars similar to GF967) in conditions of water deficit is advantageous; TYLCV protects them from stress and does not interfere with their normal development. PMC8616339.p057.s002: Thus, taking advantage of some of the virus effects, one can envisage cultivating TYLCV-infected and TYLCV-resistant plants in countries where drought is an acute problem.

PMC8616339.p051.s001: In a previous study, we demonstrated the ability of TYLCV infection to enhance the survival to drought of TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes. PMC8616339.p051.s002: The main purpose of the current study was to compare the behavior of TYLCV-susceptible (S-967) with TYLCV-resistant (R-GF967) tomato lines grown upon water withholding. PMC8616339.p051.s003: We predicted that the presence of the virus in the resistant R-GF967 tomatoes would cause a similar level of protection against drought, remain symptomless, and yield (contrary to the infected susceptible tomatoes S-967 who will decay upon drought). PMC8616339.p051.s004: Indeed, viral infection of R-GF967 tomatoes caused an improvement in growth and survival. PMC8616339.p051.s006: RVD plants showed mild wilting and leaf yellowing only after 18 dd. PMC8616339.p051.s007: It must be mentioned that virus infection significantly improved drought tolerance of S-967 (SVD) tomatoes as well, which confirmed our previous results.

PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction. PMC8616339.p007.s005: Capturing HSFA2 by viral proteins inhibited its translocation into nuclei and, consequently, downregulated the transcriptional activation of heat stress response genes. PMC8616339.p007.s006: In TYLCV-infected tomatoes exposed to drought, the activation of HSFA2 was reduced, and two other key stress transcription factors, HSFA1 and HSFB1, were also mitigated. PMC8616339.p007.s007: However, a major effect of TYLCV was the reallocation of some dominant carbohydrates and amino acids from shoots to roots, which prepared them to the effects of drought.

events.event_label: TYLCV infection induces soluble sugars and amino acids that confer drought tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p053.s001: TYLCV infection induces drought tolerance in TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes, S-967 line in this study, and in cv. PMC8616339.p053.s002: Ikram. PMC8616339.p053.s003: Moreover, this study showed that the induction of drought tolerance is particularly pronounced in infected TYLCV-resistant plants (R-GF967), which accumulate less amounts of virus than susceptible plants, thus not affecting plant growth and development. PMC8616339.p053.s004: Our findings showed that stress-protective soluble sugars and amino acids in the leaves of uninfected, and especially in virus-infected R-GF967 tomatoes, resulted in the development of a buffering state, resulting in plant protection against drought during a prolonged time (Figure 1). PMC8616339.p053.s005: Therefore, we propose that the establishment of stress osmo-protectant stability is the main feature underlaying increased drought tolerance of TYLCV-resistant tomatoes, which is utterly improved by virus infection.

PMC8616339.p002.s006: Although in infected TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes, stress markers also show an enhanced stability, in infected TYLCV-resistant plants, water balance and osmolyte homeostasis reach particularly high levels. PMC8616339.p002.s007: These tomato plants survive long periods of time during water withholding. PMC8616339.p002.s008: However, after recovery to normal irrigation, they produce fruits which are not exposed to drought, similarly to the control plants. PMC8616339.p002.s009: Using these features, it might be possible to cultivate TYLCV-resistant plants during seasons characterized by water scarcity.

PMC8616339.p004.s001: In regions of extreme drought and heat, such as the Middle East, cultivating tomato is a real challenge. PMC8616339.p004.s002: In addition, tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV), a whitefly-transmitted begomovirus, thrives in the same regions. PMC8616339.p004.s003: Often, the combination of these biotic (TYLCV) and abiotic (heat, drought) stresses cause a yield reduction up to total loss. PMC8616339.p004.s004: Therefore, there is a need to improve the tolerance of crops to the combined effects of high temperature, drought, and insect-transmitted diseases.

PMC8616339.p008.s003: TYLCV-resistant plants, containing virus amounts that did not prevent their development and fruit production, demonstrated enhanced tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p008.s004: After prolonged growth under severe water deficit, tomatoes were able to recover and yield.

PMC8616339.p027.s001: During July–August 2020, in the Jordan Valley, TYLCV-susceptible (S-967) and TYLCV-resistant (R-GF967) tomato lines were grown in one-liter pots filled with peat moss and perlite and kept in an insect-proof greenhouse. PMC8616339.p027.s002: The seedlings were either TYLCV-inoculated using viruliferous whiteflies during 7 days (dpi) or left uninfected. PMC8616339.p027.s003: Afterwards, 30 plants (15 TYLCV-inoculated and 15 not) of each line were subjected to drought by withholding irrigation for the next 18 days (drought days, dd). PMC8616339.p027.s004: In this case, water was not supplied, the only source of water was the morning dew.

PMC8616339.p030.s001: The amounts of sucrose, glucose, and fructose were analyzed by GC-MS in leaf samples, collected from S0D, SVD, R0D, and RVD plants along the 18 dd-long experiment in Jordan. PMC8616339.p030.s002: Increased amounts of sugars were observed in all experimental plants (Figure 1A). PMC8616339.p030.s003: The levels of sucrose, glucose, and fructose in S-967- and R-GF967-uninfected leaves were comparable, while TYLCV-infected S-967 tomatoes (SVD) showed a mitigation of sugars increase very early during the water withholding growth. PMC8616339.p030.s004: In leaves of TYLCV-infected R-GF967 (RVD), such suppression was not observed, and sugars levels were similar to those of R0D, or even slightly higher.

PMC8616339.p036.s001: The patterns of the major members of the HSP family—HSP70, BiP, and HSP90—were followed in the leaves of uninfected and TYLCV-infected tomato lines, i.e., S-967 and R-GF967. PMC8616339.p036.s002: Withholding of water from S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes did not cause a significant increase in the abundances of HSPs (Figure 2). PMC8616339.p036.s003: Some increases in the amounts of HSP70 and HSP90 were observed, while patterns of BiP did not change. PMC8616339.p036.s004: The amounts of HSPs in uninfected S0D and R0D plants were comparable.

PMC8616339.p038.s001: The influence of virus infection on the whole-plant physiological parameters and water balance was previously studied in uninfected and infected TYLCV-susceptible tomato cultivar Ikram, using the high-tech Pantarray-functional-phenotyping platform located in the iCORE greenhouse (, accessed on 24 October 2021), specially designed to extract simultaneously various individual information from numerous plants, under a variety of controlled conditions (e.g., water availability, nutrient level, biomass, stress index, etc.). PMC8616339.p038.s002: In the current study, we compared quantitative physiological traits in watered TYLCV-resistant vs. TYLCV-susceptible tomato lines, infected or not (RVW, SVW; ROW, SOW), and subjected to drought or not (RVD, SVD; ROD, SOD), as tested previously in Jordan.

PMC8616339.p038.s001: The influence of virus infection on the whole-plant physiological parameters and water balance was previously studied in uninfected and infected TYLCV-susceptible tomato cultivar Ikram, using the high-tech Pantarray-functional-phenotyping platform located in the iCORE greenhouse (, accessed on 24 October 2021), specially designed to extract simultaneously various individual information from numerous plants, under a variety of controlled conditions (e.g., water availability, nutrient level, biomass, stress index, etc.). PMC8616339.p038.s002: In the current study, we compared quantitative physiological traits in watered TYLCV-resistant vs. TYLCV-susceptible tomato lines, infected or not (RVW, SVW; ROW, SOW), and subjected to drought or not (RVD, SVD; ROD, SOD), as tested previously in Jordan. PMC8616339.p038.s003: Tomatoes were grown during 14 dd in the iCORE greenhouse settings and stress-related parameters were recorded individually during the 14 dd-long experiment.

PMC8616339.p040.s001: The changes in daily transpiration of watered TYLCV-susceptible and TYLCV-resistant tomato plants (S0W and R0W) were comparable along the experimental growth period (Figure 3A). PMC8616339.p040.s002: Under drought, these two lines (S0D and R0D) also behaved quite similarly, which was not entirely expected because of the differences in the osmo-protectant patterns described above (Figure 1). PMC8616339.p040.s003: TYLCV infection led to a decrease in the transpiration in SVD, which could be explained by virus-dependent attenuation of S-967 growth. PMC8616339.p040.s004: A slower decrease in transpiration rates was detected in RVWR vs. R0W, likely because the plant development was much less dependent on the presence of the virus.

PMC8616339.p043.s001: The next physiological parameter measured was the gain of fresh weight (CPW) of the experimental plants, calculated during the entire growth period. PMC8616339.p043.s002: During normal irrigation, S0W gained more fresh weight compared to SVW plants (Figure 3D). PMC8616339.p043.s003: Comparison of R0W and RVW showed a smaller difference, as expected when comparing S0W and SVW. PMC8616339.p043.s004: In drought conditions, CPW of SVD exceeded that of S0D by approximately 17%, while the CPW of RVD enhanced that of R0D by 12% (Figure 3D).

PMC8616339.p043.s006: Furthermore, the CPW of R0D reached a plateau at 10 dd, while that of RVD, reached between 12–13 dd. PMC8616339.p043.s007: The CPW calculation suggests that, in plants exposed to drought, the gain of weight was due to virus infection, but not because of the identity of the treated tomato line.

PMC8616339.p046.s001: The root to shoot ratio (R/S) of TYLCV-infected S-967 was higher than of uninfected tomatoes already during growth under normal irrigation (Figure 4, HWS vs. VWS). PMC8616339.p046.s002: During growth under drought, R/S ratio of VDS increased, while that of HDS remained unchanged. PMC8616339.p046.s003: For R-GF967 plants, R/S ratio of VWR was higher than that of HWR. PMC8616339.p046.s004: During growth under drought, both VDR and HDR stayed approximately the same; the principal difference in allocation of biomass from shoot to root was caused by the presence of TYLCV.

PMC8616339.p050.s003: Some viruses are clearly beneficial to their infected host, protecting them from heat and/or water scarcity. PMC8616339.p050.s004: Increasing amounts of studies demonstrated the way viruses manage to increase the plant’s response to environmental stresses (reviewed in). PMC8616339.p050.s005: For example, four different RNA viruses, brome mosaic virus (BMV), cucumber mosaic virus (CMV), tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), and tobacco rattle virus (TRV), were shown to improve the infected plant’s tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p050.s006: Examples of plant beneficial trade-off from infection with potato virus X (PVX) and plum pox virus (PPV) was described for N. benthamiana and Arabidopsis grown under drought conditions. PMC8616339.p050.s007: Virus infection enhanced plant tolerance to drought by increasing salicylic acid amounts, in an abscisic acid (ABA)-independent manner.

PMC8616339.p054.s001: Daily transpiration rates of S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes were comparable (Figure 3). PMC8616339.p054.s002: TYLCV infection attenuated transpiration in a similar way in both lines, stabilizing water balance in the infected tomatoes. PMC8616339.p054.s003: The confirmation of the role of TYLCV in water balance was obtained by analyzing physiological parameters, such as E, Θcrit, and CPW (Figure 3). PMC8616339.p054.s004: Θcrit showed some benefits of RVD over SVD, while WUE was somehow better for SVW than for RVW.

PMC8616339.p113.s001: Water balance in S-967 and R-GF967 plants exposed to drought stress depends on virus infection. PMC8616339.p113.s002: Using the Plantarray system, physiological parameters were measured over 14 days for uninfected normally irrigated R-GF967 (R0W) and drought-exposed (R0D) tomatoes; the same conditions were analyzed for TYLCV-infected R-GF967 plants (RVW vs. RVD). relations.subject: drought

evidence.text: This phenomenon is based on the TYLCV-dependent stabilization of amounts of key osmoprotectants induced by drought (soluble sugars, amino acids, and proteins).

relations.evidence_preview: This phenomenon is based on the TYLCV-dependent stabilization of amounts of key osmoprotectants induced by drought (soluble sugars, amino acids, and proteins).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p002.s003: To envisage the use of virus protective capacity in agriculture, TYLCV-resistant tomato lines have to be infected first with the virus before planting. PMC8616339.p002.s004: Such virus-resistant tomato plants contain virus amounts that do not cause disease symptoms, growth inhibition, or yield loss, but are sufficient to modify the metabolism of the plant, resulting in improved tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p002.s005: This phenomenon is based on the TYLCV-dependent stabilization of amounts of key osmoprotectants induced by drought (soluble sugars, amino acids, and proteins). PMC8616339.p002.s006: Although in infected TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes, stress markers also show an enhanced stability, in infected TYLCV-resistant plants, water balance and osmolyte homeostasis reach particularly high levels. PMC8616339.p002.s007: These tomato plants survive long periods of time during water withholding.

relations.subject: extreme drought

evidence.text: In regions of extreme drought and heat, such as the Middle East, cultivating tomato is a real challenge.

relations.evidence_preview: In regions of extreme drought and heat, such as the Middle East, cultivating tomato is a real challenge.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p004.s001: In regions of extreme drought and heat, such as the Middle East, cultivating tomato is a real challenge. PMC8616339.p004.s002: In addition, tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV), a whitefly-transmitted begomovirus, thrives in the same regions. PMC8616339.p004.s003: Often, the combination of these biotic (TYLCV) and abiotic (heat, drought) stresses cause a yield reduction up to total loss. PMC8616339.p004.s004: Therefore, there is a need to improve the tolerance of crops to the combined effects of high temperature, drought, and insect-transmitted diseases.

relations.subject: proteins

evidence.text: It was proposed that one of the potential mechanisms of suppression of heat stress response was based on the ability of all six viral proteins to interact with the main heat shock transcription factor HSFA2.

relations.evidence_preview: It was proposed that one of the potential mechanisms of suppression of heat stress response was based on the ability of all six viral proteins to interact with the main heat shock transcription factor HSFA2.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p007.s001: TYLCV is able to manipulate the plant heat stress response and moderate the effects caused by heat on the plant. PMC8616339.p007.s002: It was proposed that one of the potential mechanisms of suppression of heat stress response was based on the ability of all six viral proteins to interact with the main heat shock transcription factor HSFA2. PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p007.s001: TYLCV is able to manipulate the plant heat stress response and moderate the effects caused by heat on the plant. PMC8616339.p007.s002: It was proposed that one of the potential mechanisms of suppression of heat stress response was based on the ability of all six viral proteins to interact with the main heat shock transcription factor HSFA2. PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction.

relations.subject: HSFA2

evidence.text: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction.

relations.evidence_preview: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p007.s003: In tomato cell cultures subjected to heat stress, HSFA2 accumulated to high levels during prolonged exposition to heat. PMC8616339.p007.s004: HSFA2 regulated the expression of members of the ascorbate peroxidase (APX) family, which are major scavengers of intercellular H2O2 and prevented ROS overproduction. PMC8616339.p007.s005: Capturing HSFA2 by viral proteins inhibited its translocation into nuclei and, consequently, downregulated the transcriptional activation of heat stress response genes. PMC8616339.p007.s006: In TYLCV-infected tomatoes exposed to drought, the activation of HSFA2 was reduced, and two other key stress transcription factors, HSFA1 and HSFB1, were also mitigated. PMC8616339.p007.s007: However, a major effect of TYLCV was the reallocation of some dominant carbohydrates and amino acids from shoots to roots, which prepared them to the effects of drought.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p031.s001: The levels of several amino acids, known to be involved in response to drought, were analyzed by GC-MS, and compared between leaves of S-967 and R-GF967 tomatoes exposed to drought during the 18 days-long experiment. PMC8616339.p031.s002: The amounts of proline, HO-proline, tryptophan, GABA, glutamine, valine, asparagine, and methionine were compared in S0D and SVD vs. R0D and RVD (Figure 1B). PMC8616339.p031.s003: These selected amino acids were induced by drought to varying degrees and at different periods during exposure to drought. PMC8616339.p031.s004: For example, the amounts of proline and of its derivative HO-proline increased during the entire 18-dd growth period.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p031.s006: A surprising result was obtained in uninfected R-GF967 tomatoes, where levels of proline and HO-proline exceeded that in non-infected S-967 (R0D vs. S0D). PMC8616339.p031.s007: The quantities of γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), another osmolyte protecting cells against stresses, increased upon drought but only after 12 dd (Figure 1B). PMC8616339.p031.s008: Alike-proline and HO-proline GABA amounts were higher, not only in RVD vs. SVD, but also in R0D vs. S0D. PMC8616339.p031.s009: Besides GABA, three other members of glutamate family, glutamate, glutamine, and arginine, were analyzed. PMC8616339.p031.s010: Glutamic acid was not induced by drought (not shown), similar to the absence of drought-dependent induction of this amino acid in the tomato commercial cultivar Ikram, contradicting data in the literature.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p050.s003: Some viruses are clearly beneficial to their infected host, protecting them from heat and/or water scarcity. PMC8616339.p050.s004: Increasing amounts of studies demonstrated the way viruses manage to increase the plant’s response to environmental stresses (reviewed in). PMC8616339.p050.s005: For example, four different RNA viruses, brome mosaic virus (BMV), cucumber mosaic virus (CMV), tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), and tobacco rattle virus (TRV), were shown to improve the infected plant’s tolerance to drought. PMC8616339.p050.s006: Examples of plant beneficial trade-off from infection with potato virus X (PVX) and plum pox virus (PPV) was described for N. benthamiana and Arabidopsis grown under drought conditions. PMC8616339.p050.s007: Virus infection enhanced plant tolerance to drought by increasing salicylic acid amounts, in an abscisic acid (ABA)-independent manner.

evidence.text: In a previous study, we demonstrated the ability of TYLCV infection to enhance the survival to drought of TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC8616339.p051.s001: In a previous study, we demonstrated the ability of TYLCV infection to enhance the survival to drought of TYLCV-susceptible tomatoes. PMC8616339.p051.s002: The main purpose of the current study was to compare the behavior of TYLCV-susceptible (S-967) with TYLCV-resistant (R-GF967) tomato lines grown upon water withholding. PMC8616339.p051.s003: We predicted that the presence of the virus in the resistant R-GF967 tomatoes would cause a similar level of protection against drought, remain symptomless, and yield (contrary to the infected susceptible tomatoes S-967 who will decay upon drought). PMC8616339.p051.s004: Indeed, viral infection of R-GF967 tomatoes caused an improvement in growth and survival.
